title: Short Curves of End-Periodic Mapping Tori
speaker: Brandis Whitfield
abstract:
Fibered 3-manifolds are those constructed via surface homeomorphisms. Given such a manifold with pseudo-Anosov monodromy, much is already known about how topological data of the mapping class determine geometric information about the hyperbolic 3-manifold. Currently, there is a lot of research activity surrounding the mapping tori of end-periodic homeomorphisms of infinite-type surfaces. 
As an "infinite type" analogue to work of Minsky in the finite-type setting, we show that given a subsurface Y of S, the subsurface projections between the "positive" and "negative" Handel-Miller laminations provide bounds for the geodesic length of the boundary of Y as it resides in the end-periodic mapping torus. 
In this talk, we'll discuss the motivating theory for finite-type surfaces and closed fibered hyperbolic 3-manifolds, show these techniques may be used in the infinite-type setting, and how our main theorems return results to the closed, fibered setting.
tags: Joint IAS/PU Groups and Dynamics Seminar

proof_stages.label: Finite-type mapping tori and lamination geometry
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls the finite-type benchmark: a closed-surface mapping torus is hyperbolic exactly for pseudo-Anosov monodromy, whose stable and unstable laminations are best measured via the coarse geometry of the arc-and-curve graph.

proof_stages.label: Subsurface projection and Minsky short-curve criterion
proof_stages.summary: Since the stable and unstable laminations intersect too much globally, they are projected to an essential subsurface, and Minsky's theorem says that large projection distance forces the geodesic boundary of that subsurface to be short in the hyperbolic mapping torus.

proof_stages.label: End-periodic infinite-type mapping tori
proof_stages.summary: The finite-type story is shifted to infinite-type surfaces with finitely many nonplanar ends, where end-periodic homeomorphisms are defined by attracting and repelling neighborhoods and have tame mapping tori obtained by compactifying flow lines.

proof_stages.label: Atoroidal and acylindrical hypotheses for hyperbolization
proof_stages.summary: To obtain useful hyperbolic structures, periodic curves are excluded to remove essential tori and strong irreducibility excludes reducing curves and periodic arcs to remove essential annuli, leading in the acylindrical case to a distinguished hyperbolic metric with totally geodesic boundary.

proof_stages.label: Handel-Miller laminations and the main large-projection theorem
proof_stages.summary: End-periodic maps have invariant Handel-Miller laminations, and the main theorem asserts a Minsky-type implication: for atoroidal maps of bounded capacity genus, sufficiently large finite-type subsurface projection gives a hyperbolic structure in which the subsurface boundary is short.

proof_stages.label: Closed fibered hyperbolic corollary with short totally geodesic surfaces
proof_stages.summary: The infinite-type theorem is applied back to finite-type topology to produce, for any genus and epsilon, a closed fibered hyperbolic three-manifold containing a totally geodesic genus-g surface whose systole is less than epsilon.

examples.name: Compactly supported map on the ladder surface
examples.note: The compactly supported homeomorphism leaves an infinite product piece in the mapping torus, motivating the move to genuinely end-periodic maps to obtain tame manifolds.

passes.reason: A compactly supported map is trivial near the infinite ends and is presented as a bad case whose mapping torus is not tame/finitely generated.

examples.name: Periodic curve producing a torus
examples.note: A curve preserved up to finite power glues to a circle times a circle in the mapping torus, revealing why periodic curves are precisely an atoroidal obstruction.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker traces a periodic curve through the mapping-torus gluing and explains how the curve’s orbit produces a torus
